markers.whatItMeasures: Cells in your blood that help fight infections and heal injuries.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand immune system activity.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be influenced by various factors. Your clinician can help determine what's appropriate for you.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be influenced by various factors including recent illness or stress. Your clinician can provide context.

markers.whatItMeasures: Cells that carry oxygen throughout your body.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand oxygen transport capacity.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors. Often reviewed alongside iron markers.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Your clinician can provide context.

markers.whatItMeasures: The protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand how well oxygen is transported in the blood.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various nutritional factors. Often discussed alongside iron status.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Your clinician can provide context.

markers.whatItMeasures: The percentage of your blood made up of red blood cells.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked alongside hemoglobin to understand blood composition.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors. Often reviewed with hemoglobin.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Often reviewed with hemoglobin.

markers.whatItMeasures: The average size of your red blood cells.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to provide context about red blood cell characteristics.
markers.belowContext: Smaller cells may be associated with various nutritional factors. Often discussed with iron status.
markers.aboveContext: Larger cells may be associated with various nutritional factors. Often discussed with B12 and folate.

markers.whatItMeasures: The average amount of hemoglobin in each red blood cell.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked alongside MCV to understand red blood cell characteristics.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various nutritional factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various nutritional factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The average concentration of hemoglobin in red blood cells.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked alongside other red blood cell markers.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A measure of variation in red blood cell size.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to provide additional context about red blood cells.
markers.belowContext: Lower variation may indicate more uniform cell sizes.
markers.aboveContext: Higher variation may be associated with various nutritional factors. Often discussed with iron, B12, and folate.

markers.whatItMeasures: Cells that help your blood clot and stop bleeding.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand blood clotting function.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors. Your clinician can provide context.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Your clinician can provide context.

markers.whatItMeasures: A type of white blood cell that fights bacterial infections.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of understanding immune function.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors. Your clinician can provide context.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with recent infection or stress.

markers.whatItMeasures: White blood cells that help fight viral infections.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of understanding immune function.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with recent viral illness.

markers.whatItMeasures: White blood cells that help clean up infections and damaged cells.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of the white blood cell differential.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: White blood cells involved in allergic responses and fighting parasites.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of the white blood cell differential.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common and often not concerning.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with allergies or other factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: White blood cells involved in allergic and inflammatory responses.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of the white blood cell differential.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: Blood sugar level after not eating for 8-12 hours.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand how the body manages blood sugar.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with timing of meals or other factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary patterns and lifestyle factors. Often reviewed over time.

markers.whatItMeasures: Average blood sugar level over the past 2-3 months.
markers.whyTested: Provides a longer-term view of blood sugar patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels generally reflect lower average blood sugar.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary and lifestyle patterns over time.

markers.whatItMeasures: A hormone that helps move sugar from blood into cells.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to provide context about blood sugar management.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary and lifestyle patterns.

markers.whatItMeasures: A waste product filtered by the kidneys.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand kidney function patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various nutritional factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with hydration status or protein intake.

markers.whatItMeasures: A waste product from muscle filtered by the kidneys.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand kidney function patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with muscle mass or other factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Often reviewed with BUN and eGFR.

markers.whatItMeasures: An estimate of how well the kidneys are filtering.
markers.whyTested: Calculated from creatinine to understand kidney function.
markers.belowContext: Lower values may be associated with various factors affecting kidney function.
markers.aboveContext: Higher values generally indicate better filtering function.

markers.whatItMeasures: An important mineral for fluid balance and nerve function.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of electrolyte balance.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with hydration patterns or other factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with hydration status or dietary sodium intake.

markers.whatItMeasures: An important mineral for heart rhythm and muscle function.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of electrolyte balance.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake or other factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary intake or other factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An electrolyte that helps maintain fluid balance.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked alongside other electrolytes.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A measure related to acid-base balance in the body.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of electrolyte and metabolic panels.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various metabolic factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A mineral important for bones, muscles, and nerves.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand calcium status.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake or vitamin D status.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The total amount of protein in your blood.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand overall protein status.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary protein intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with hydration status or other factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The main protein made by the liver.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand protein status and liver function.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary protein intake or other factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with hydration status.

markers.whatItMeasures: Proteins in blood including antibodies and other important proteins.
markers.whyTested: Calculated from total protein and albumin.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A substance produced when red blood cells break down.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand liver function and red blood cell turnover.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Often reviewed with liver enzymes.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme found mainly in the liver.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand liver function patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various lifestyle factors. Often reviewed with other liver markers.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme found in liver, heart, and muscle tissue.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand liver and muscle function patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors. Often reviewed with ALT.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme found in liver and bone.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand liver and bone function patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with bone growth or other factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The total amount of cholesterol in your blood.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of understanding lipid patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary and lifestyle patterns. Often reviewed with HDL, LDL, and triglycerides.

markers.whatItMeasures: One type of cholesterol often discussed in wellness contexts.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of lipid patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes discussed in wellness contexts.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary and lifestyle patterns.

markers.whatItMeasures: One type of cholesterol often discussed in wellness contexts.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of lipid patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with lifestyle patterns.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are sometimes discussed in wellness contexts.

markers.whatItMeasures: A type of fat in the blood that provides energy.
markers.whyTested: Commonly reviewed as part of lipid patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary patterns, especially refined carbohydrates and alcohol. Fasting status before the test matters.

markers.whatItMeasures: A type of cholesterol that carries triglycerides.
markers.whyTested: Usually calculated from triglycerides.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with triglyceride patterns.

markers.whatItMeasures: Total cholesterol minus HDL cholesterol.
markers.whyTested: Calculated to provide additional lipid context.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes discussed.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary and lifestyle patterns.

markers.whatItMeasures: A protein component of certain cholesterol particles.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to provide additional lipid context.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes discussed.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary and lifestyle patterns.

markers.whatItMeasures: A type of cholesterol particle largely influenced by genetics.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked as part of advanced lipid evaluation.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are often genetically influenced.

markers.whatItMeasures: A hormone that signals the thyroid gland.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand thyroid function patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors affecting thyroid activity.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors affecting thyroid activity.

markers.whatItMeasures: The active form of a major thyroid hormone in the blood.
markers.whyTested: Often checked to provide additional thyroid context when TSH is abnormal.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various thyroid-related factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various thyroid-related factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The active form of another thyroid hormone.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to provide comprehensive thyroid context.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An inactive form of thyroid hormone.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked in specialized thyroid evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with stress or calorie restriction.

markers.whatItMeasures: The amount of iron currently in your blood.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand iron status.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary iron intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A protein that stores iron in your body.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand iron storage status.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with iron intake over time.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A measure of the blood's capacity to carry iron.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked alongside iron and ferritin.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with iron status.

markers.whatItMeasures: The percentage of iron-binding sites that are filled.
markers.whyTested: Calculated from iron and TIBC to understand iron availability.
markers.belowContext: Lower percentages may be associated with iron availability.
markers.aboveContext: Higher percentages may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The main form of vitamin D in the blood.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand vitamin D status.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common and may be associated with limited sun exposure or dietary intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are sometimes seen with supplementation.

markers.whatItMeasures: A vitamin important for nerves and blood cells.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand B12 status.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake, especially in vegetarians and vegans.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are sometimes seen with supplementation.

markers.whatItMeasures: A B vitamin important for cell growth and blood cells.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked alongside B12.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are sometimes seen with supplementation.

markers.whatItMeasures: A protein that rises in response to inflammation.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand inflammatory patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels generally indicate less inflammation.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with recent illness, injury, or various lifestyle factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A more sensitive measure of inflammation.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes used to understand subtle inflammatory patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels generally indicate less inflammation.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various lifestyle and dietary factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A measure of how quickly red blood cells settle.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to understand inflammatory patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The main male hormone, also present in smaller amounts in females.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to understand hormone patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various lifestyle and age-related factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The active, unbound form of testosterone.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to provide additional hormone context.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: The main estrogen hormone.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to understand hormone patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various age-related and lifestyle factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A hormone related to stress response.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked to understand stress hormone patterns.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with stress, sleep patterns, or testing time of day.

markers.whatItMeasures: A hormone that can be converted to other hormones.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked as part of hormone evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with age or stress patterns.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An amino acid in the blood.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked as part of cardiovascular or nutritional evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are generally common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with B vitamin status or other factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A substance produced when the body breaks down purines.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked as part of metabolic panels.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with dietary purine intake or other factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A measure of how long blood takes to clot.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand clotting function.
markers.belowContext: Faster clotting may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Slower clotting may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: Another measure of blood clotting time.
markers.whyTested: Commonly checked to understand clotting function.
markers.belowContext: Faster clotting may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Slower clotting may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A standardized way of reporting PT results.
markers.whyTested: Used to monitor certain situations requiring specific clotting times.
markers.belowContext: Lower values indicate faster clotting.
markers.aboveContext: Higher values indicate slower clotting.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme found in muscles and heart.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked after muscle injury or intense exercise.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with recent intense exercise or muscle stress.

markers.whatItMeasures: A protein found in heart muscle.
markers.whyTested: Checked in specific clinical contexts related to heart muscle.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are typical.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may indicate heart muscle stress.

markers.whatItMeasures: A protein released by the heart.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked in specific clinical contexts.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are typical.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various cardiac factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A hormone that regulates calcium and phosphorus.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked when calcium or vitamin D are abnormal.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with various factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with calcium or vitamin D status.

markers.whatItMeasures: A mineral important for muscles, nerves, and bones.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked as part of metabolic evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are less common.

markers.whatItMeasures: A mineral important for bones and energy.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked alongside calcium.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake or other factors.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: A mineral important for immune function and healing.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked in nutritional evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels are sometimes seen with supplementation.

markers.whatItMeasures: A mineral involved in various body processes.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked alongside zinc.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels may be associated with dietary intake.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme that helps digest fats.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked in digestive evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various digestive factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme that helps digest carbohydrates.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked in digestive evaluations.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are sometimes seen.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme found in the liver and bile ducts.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked alongside other liver enzymes.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with various lifestyle factors.

markers.whatItMeasures: An enzyme found in many body tissues.
markers.whyTested: Sometimes checked when tissue damage is suspected.
markers.belowContext: Lower levels are common.
markers.aboveContext: Higher levels may be associated with tissue stress or recent intense exercise.
